medications.indication_rationale: Oral non-sedating antihistamine blocks the histaminergic itch of the local reaction. A stat dose gives prompt symptomatic relief; it is an over-the-counter pharmacy medicine the patient can obtain without a prescription.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally, stat then once daily as needed

medications.indication_rationale: An equally acceptable non-sedating antihistamine for ongoing histaminergic pruritus over the next few days; interchangeable with cetirizine or loratadine per patient preference/availability.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 180 mg orally once daily for 3 days

medications.indication_rationale: A short course of a moderate-potency topical corticosteroid suppresses the local inflammatory infiltrate and speeds settling of the wheal and itch. Applied thinly to the lesion only, for a limited course. This is a prescription item a telehealth clinician can supply.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply thinly to the lesion twice daily for up to 5 days
medications.deprescribing_note: Stop after 5 days; do not continue potent topical steroid on the area beyond the short course.

medications.indication_rationale: There is no evidence of bacterial infection (no spreading erythema, lymphangitis, fever or pain-predominance). Prescribing antibiotics for an uninfected bite reaction is an error of commission and poor antimicrobial stewardship — antibiotics are reserved for if genuine cellulitis develops (see safety-netting).
A benign localised bite reaction does not warrant systemic steroids; the risk-benefit does not support them for this self-limiting presentation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply a cold compress (an ice pack or bag of frozen peas wrapped in a damp towel) to the lump for about 15 minutes whenever the itch becomes overwhelming.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cold numbs local nerve endings and vasoconstricts, giving physical itch relief without breaking the skin.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do not scratch the bite; keep fingernails short and cover the lesion if needed. Wash the area gently with soap and water once daily.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Scratching causes micro-tears that let skin bacteria in and can turn a benign reaction into a secondary bacterial infection (cellulitis).

patient_education_points.point: This is your immune system over-reacting to the insect's saliva, releasing histamine into the skin, which causes the hard swelling and intense itch. It is a benign, localised reaction — not a venomous emergency or an allergic reaction affecting your whole body.

The antihistamine tablets calm the itch from the inside and the steroid ointment acts like a fire-extinguisher on the local skin inflammation. The itch and hard swelling should ease noticeably over the next 24-48 hours and the lump should settle over a few days.
Please try hard not to scratch it — scratching can introduce bacteria and turn this into a skin infection that would need antibiotics.
Stop the steroid ointment after 5 days.

follow_up_plan.interval: review with your regular GP in about 3 days if it is not settling, and sooner if any warning signs appear
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: lesion size and firmness, any spread of redness, signs of infection, and adequacy of itch control
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: spreading redness or a red streak up the leg, the area becoming hot and increasingly painful rather than itchy, fever or chills, or any breathing/swelling/whole-body allergic symptoms

must_include_items: Recognise this as a benign, localised insect-bite reaction after screening the dangerous mimics — not a venomous emergency or systemic anaphylaxis
Recommend an oral non-sedating antihistamine for the itch (pharmacy-available)
Advise symptomatic skin care: cold compress for itch, do NOT scratch, gentle daily washing
Provide safety-netting: call 000 for any evolving anaphylaxis; seek prompt/urgent review for spreading infection (cellulitis)
Reassure and keep management in the community — do not over-triage to ED
acceptable_alternatives: Any non-sedating antihistamine (cetirizine, fexofenadine or loratadine) is acceptable
A short course of a moderate-potency topical corticosteroid is ideal but managing with antihistamine plus supportive care and safety-netting alone is also an acceptable pass
Directing the patient to a pharmacist for antihistamine and advice is acceptable for this low-acuity presentation
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for evolving anaphylaxis (000)
Failing to safety-net for spreading cellulitis (prompt review)
Failing to advise against scratching / wound hygiene
Failing to offer any itch relief
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging a well patient with a benign localised bite reaction to ED or advising 000 for the current presentation
Prescribing oral antibiotics for an uninfected bite
Being pulled into the patient's spider-bite/anaphylaxis panic and over-investigating
Dismissing the presentation with no safety-netting at all
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass provides symptomatic itch management (antihistamine, with or without a topical steroid) PLUS clear conservative safety-netting for the two must-not-miss mimics (evolving anaphylaxis -> 000; spreading cellulitis -> prompt/urgent review), while keeping disposition in the community. This is a low-acuity case: over-triage to ED/000 for the current benign presentation is a defect, but the more serious failure is omitting the anaphylaxis/cellulitis safety-net.
